explicit_text: My name is Miyake Kiyo, born in 1898 in Senin, Hokkaido. I grew up in a Christian family where my father was a minister, and I attended Osaka Jogakuin, then known as Wilmina Jogakuin. I was deeply influenced by my parents, especially my mother, who was a great teacher and confidant to me. Over the years, I experienced many challenges, including my father's illness and my own struggles, but I always held on to the values they taught me. Later, I moved to America, influenced by my desire to continue studying and my westernized thinking.

explicit_text: I was born in Senin, Hokkaido, where my father initially worked as an organizing minister. He had a vision to create a Christian model farm in Hokkaido by the Ishikawa River, funded by a benefactor related to Mr. Takechi and Mr. Ishimaru in Stockton. Unfortunately, the project ended when the benefactor died. Hokkaido was a unique place for me, and I recall singing songs related to the region during the Nishin Senso period.

explicit_text: My father worked in Yamada, Iwate, where he faced the challenge of building a church from scratch. Despite the difficulty, within two years he successfully established a church with over 100 members. Missionaries like Mrs. Helport and Miss Riker supported the ministry by running a kindergarten. Yamada was a tough place to evangelize, but it produced many young ministers under my father's influence.

I lived in the dormitory there for four years and was deeply involved in school activities.
